fragments.content: Generic drugs are copies of brand-name drugs that have the same active ingredient, dosage form, strength, route of administration, and intended use. The Abbreviated New Drug Application (ANDA) pathway, established by the Hatch-Waxman Act of 1984, allows generic approval without repeating clinical efficacy trials if bioequivalence to the reference listed drug (RLD) is demonstrated. Generics must be therapeutically equivalent (same clinical effect and safety profile). Upon approval, generics receive an 'AB' therapeutic equivalence rating in the Orange Book, indicating they can be substituted for the brand at the pharmacy level.

ANDA submission requirements include: demonstration of bioequivalence (typically fasting pharmacokinetic study in healthy volunteers showing 90% CI for AUC and Cmax within 80-125% of RLD), same active ingredient, dosage form, strength, and route of administration as RLD, labeling essentially identical to RLD (except differences due to patent protection or exclusivity), and compliance with cGMP manufacturing standards. Bioequivalence studies measure rate and extent of absorption; for narrow therapeutic index drugs, tighter limits may apply. Certain products require additional studies (food-effect, steady-state, clinical endpoint studies for locally acting drugs). FDA reviews ANDAs for scientific, quality, and labeling adequacy.

Paragraph IV certification is a legal challenge asserting that patents listed in the Orange Book for the RLD are invalid, unenforceable, or will not be infringed by the generic. First applicant to file a substantially complete ANDA with Paragraph IV certification receives 180 days of marketing exclusivity against other generics, creating significant financial incentive. This often triggers patent infringement litigation by brand manufacturers, with 30-month stay of generic approval while litigation proceeds (if brand sues within 45 days). Settlements sometimes involve 'pay-for-delay' agreements where brand pays generic to delay launch, which face antitrust scrutiny. Generic approval criteria: bioequivalence established, no unresolved patent/exclusivity barriers, adequate manufacturing quality. Generics typically cost 80-85% less than brand names after multiple competitors enter.
